title: Letters to the Editor
summary: DESCRIPTION: A Cup'ik Eskimo village that maintains reindeer and musk ox herds, and where almost all families engage in subsistence activities and most have fish camps. Some jobs are available through the school (attended by about 40 students), city, village corporation, construction projects and services. Trapping and crafts, such as knitting qiviut (musk ox underwool), provide income for many families, and more than 50 residents hold commercial fishing permits. HISTORY: Nunivak Island has been inhabited for 2,000 years by the Nuniwarmiut people, or Cup'ik Eskimos. First outside contact was in 1821 by the Russian American Co., whose members recorded 400 people living in 16 villages on the Island. A summer camp, "Koot," was noted at the current site of Mekoryuk in 1874. An epidemic in 1900…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: DESCRIPTION: A traditional Tlingit village known for its Chilkat blankets and dance robes woven from mountain goat hair and cedar bark. It is the only inland settlement in Southeast Alaska, accessible from the Haines Highway, which is connected to the Alaska Highway through Canada. Fishing, logging and subsistence activities support the community. Besides blanket weaving, several residents also make jewelry and sew moccasins. The area hosts the largest concentration of bald eagles in the world at the Chilkat Bald Eagle Reserve. Residents subsist on salmon and eulachon from the Chilkat River, and they also harvest halibut, shellfish, deer, mountain sheep, bear and berries. There is a single school, attended by about 40 students. HISTORY: In 1880, the U.S. Navy reported the name of the villa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Good Neighbors (Published: December 28, 2004) William Pugh's kindergarten class at Muldoon Elementary School collected 279 items during the school's 2004 canned food drive. A total of 2,161 items were brought in during the three-week effort. The top-producing class brought in more than 700 items. Dean Brovald, left, and John Weddleton Click on photo to enlarge * THE GREAT CATALOG ROUNDUP, held Nov. 13 at the Dimond Center, involved more than 850 participants who collected 2,100 pounds of catalogs for recycling. Green Star worked with Buy Alaska (a program of the Alaska Small Business Development Center), Alaskans for Litter Prevention and Recycling and the Dimond Center to trade catalogs for cash. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "They concluded I was baiting the moose," Napolilli said. "I've been storing hay in that barn for 35 years." Lt. Gary Folger of the Alaska Bureau of Wildlife Enforcement said such a citation is not unprecedented. Two were issued last year. "The bottom line is many people don't realize hay is not good for moose," Folger said. Napolilli, a retired high school teacher, has requested a court date. He said troopers are misreading the law that prohibits the intentional leaving of human food, human waste, garbage or pet food to attract wildlife. "I'm obviously not trying to attract wildlife, and it's not pet food," he said. Napolilli said his moose encounter began about 10 a.m. Wednesday when his 14-month-old German shepherd, Geyer, began barking furiously. Napolilli looked out his window and saw…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: What Gary means is those senators who pause periodically during recitation of the pledge of allegiance to the flag each morning and those who barrel through taking only one breath, or maybe none. Huh, you might say? It seems the senators take turns leading the pledge. Some pause. Some don't. With just a glance at the handy-dandy cards, the rest of the chamber knows whether to say, "IpledgeallegiancetotheflagoftheUnitedStatesofAmericaandtotheRepublicforwhichitstandsonenationunderGodindivisiblewithlibertyandjusticeforall." Or: "I pledge allegiance to the flag (pause) of the United States of America (pause) and to the Republic (pause) for which it stands (pause) one nation (pause) under God (pause) indivisible with liberty and justice for all." Or something like that. Earwigs report Wilken th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Everyone expects the fight to go back to the courts if the Legislature passes the bill. The ruling that made at-home pot possession of up to four ounces legal for personal use was based on the right to privacy in the state constitution. The Legislature cannot change the constitution without a statewide vote. But the governor hopes the bill and hearings over it will show the courts that pot is a lot more powerful than it used to be and that the state has an overriding interest in forbidding it. William Satterberg, the Fairbanks lawyer who argued the case that toppled the state prohibition on at-home pot, said he doesn't think the courts will backtrack. "Unconstitutional still remains unconstitutional no matter what the Legislature thinks," Satterberg said. The Alaska Supreme Court in Septem…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "The main purpose of the camp was to create economic opportunities for shareholders on Nunivak Island," said camp manager Terry Don. "We are looking to expand our operations next year. We hope to double or triple that number" of students. The program included lectures, day hiking, backpacking and sea kayaking to bolster classroom instruction. The academic staff included Native elders who supplemented scientific knowledge with local and indigenous knowledge of Nunivak Island. The core curriculum of the course is a blend of Western academic and traditional subsistence sciences. Field projects are designed around real-time needs for data for the community and the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, as well as for long-term scientific efforts at the wildlife refuge. "NIMA did a great job pulling t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "A hundred years later the whole picture is significantly more complex," said Dale Bosworth, the Forest Service's chief. "The population has increased significantly. There are much more demands than there were 100 years ago in terms of recreation and solitude." The forest system faces a host of troubles, from overgrown forests that contribute to major fires to the spread of invasive species and urban encroachment on open space. Arguments over proper management have erupted on many fronts. In December the Bush administration released rules for the national forests that some environmentalists complain relax ecology-friendly restrictions and make it easier to log public lands. Former Forest Service Chief Jack Ward Thomas said Roosevelt and Pinchot would hardly recognize the agency they create…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Study touts benefits of new center CONVENTIONS: Economic analysis commissioned by group in favor of the project. By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 12, 2005) Click on photo to enlarge A new convention center in downtown Anchorage could add 1,925 jobs and $133.2 million in spending to the city's economy by its fifth year of operation, an economic study found. Pat Burden, president of Anchorage-based Northern Economics, presented some of the study's key findings during a press briefing Tuesday. The study was commissioned by a political nonprofit group that's aiming to persuade voters to increase the city's hotel tax to 12 percent from 8 percent. The extra cash would fund a new convention center. Anchorage Civic and Convention Center Yes -- a privately funded group …

summary: A good magician never tells EVEN A YOUNG ONE: Magic Kids dazzle audiences around town. By KATIE PESZNECKER Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 27, 2004) Robbie Christenson, 14, pulls a watch out of an empty tube during the sixth annual Alaska Magic Kids Holiday Magic Extravaganza on Sunday at the Snow Goose Theatre. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Card tricks, crafty illusions and even a cannon captivated a young, wide-eyed audience Sunday afternoon, but the coolest part of the annual magic show is its cast: All the magicians are kids. The youths are part of Alaska Magic Kids, a group that meets monthly to learn and practice magic. The troupe performs several times a year, including at the Alaska State Fair, the Saturday Market and, for the sixth…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: "Governor, regardless of the result of your special counsel's investigation, the attorney general has, I believe, violated numerous rules of professional conduct. These violations are very public. The violations must be addressed and investigated by the Alaska Bar Association," Shea wrote. A Murkowski spokeswoman said Monday that the governor is awaiting completion of an investigation he ordered before deciding on his next step. Murkowski appointed Anchorage lawyer Robert Bundy, also a former U.S. attorney, as outside counsel in October to investigate Renkes' stock ownership in KFx Inc., a Denver company trying to commercialize a coal-drying technology. Renkes owned more than $100,000 worth of KFx stock as he crafted a trade agreement with Taiwan that could benefit the Denver company. He h…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Sen. Gene Therriault, a North Pole Republican, has also filed a bill related to a fight in the fall election. His bill would require that citizen requests for absentee ballots go straight to the Division of Elections. This comes after the Democratic Party last fall put its own return address, instead of the Elections Division's, on applications for absentee ballots it gave to voters. Republicans said that gave Democrats access to private information and control over whether to forward the requests. The elections bills are two of the 20 bills filed Friday in advance of the 2005 legislative session. Others included measures to require a public vote before Juneau can build a new state Capitol building; to give each local government $50,000; and even "an Act relating to the transportation of d…

title: Bicycle-tire studs are not created equal
summary: Sometimes the good deal isn't the best buy. The third smack-down on my mountain bike was enough to remind me of that axiom. Goldenview Drive had proven wet and slushy, but easily rideable. Bragaw Street had slippery spots, but nothing scary. The crossing from there onto Abbott Loop Road behind the Section 16 equestrian park had been a push through crusty old snow. Then the ride got crazy. Abbott Loop was a skating rink all the way to its intersection with Abbott Road. I no sooner got on the bike than it went down. The first fall was unexpected. So were the next two. On studded bike tires, it is usually possible to ride over almost anything. Only later would come the discovery that my cheap studded tires weren't really studded anymore. Not to imply that this good deal had been an inherently…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Wolf killing slow so far in predator control program The Associated Press FAIRBANKS - Bad weather, little daylight and lack of snow have conspired to frustrate hunters participating in the state's aerial wolf control programs. The harvest as of Friday in four regions open to aerial wolf hunting was 51 wolves, according to the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. Pilot-gunner teams have reported taking 21 wolves from Game Management Unit 13 in the Nelchina Basin, 25 in Unit 16B west of Cook Inlet and five in McGrath's Unit 19D East. No wolves have been killed in Unit 19A in the central Kuskokwim River area. "It's probably down from what we'd like to see but given the circumstances it's probably the best we could hope for," said Bruce Bartley, a department information officer in Anchorage. La…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: It was almost like being home at Alyeska Resort. Elsewhere, it was decidedly more cosmopolitan. On the lower lifts, there were a lot of Australian beginners and intermediates, wrestling with icy terrain. Mixed in were Japanese, Koreans and a few Chinese skiers, who seemed to be having the same problems. Many didn't speak English, and I am sadly limited to that language, so conversation was impossible. The others, the Aussies, the Scotsmen, the Quebecers who moved easily between French and English, the Englishmen, and the New Zealanders were more talkative. Capable skiers, the Kiwis were up high, which means more than a mile above the valley, scratching away on the black diamond runs. It was not great skiing. The steepest runs were heavily moguled and icy. Worse than that, Whistler is behin…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Mat-Su to host free Winter Trails Day event Matanuska-Susitna Borough Recreational Services Division, the Mat-Su Trails Council and Alaska Trails will put on a free Winter Trails Day event from 11 a.m. to 1 p.m. Saturday at the new ski area parking lot at Mile 10.6 of Hatcher Pass Road. A snowshoe demonstration is the highlight of the event. Winter Trails Day is part of a national public awareness campaign called "Winter Feels Good," developed to promote the health, fitness and social benefits of snow sports. Snowshoes will be provided, and there will be hot drinks for warming up. For more information, call 1-907-745-9690. SKIJORING Mushing club sponsors skijor races at the Aurora Dog Mushing Trails Aurora Dog Mushing Club sponsors a one- and two-dog skijor race beginning Saturday at the A…

summary: Retail chains sprout beyond big city GROWTH: For first time since '99, a big-box store won't open in Anchorage. The Home Depot's newly constructed store sits on a bluff overlooking the Parks Highway and Wasilla Lake. (Photo by Stephen Nowers / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge In 2004, big-box retailers turned their attention away from Anchorage to Alaska's other large communities, bringing huge buildings and hundreds of new jobs to Fairbanks and the Matanuska Valley. For the first time since 1999, a big-box store has not opened in Anchorage, according to econom…

summary: Mayor makes yearly pitch for funds WISH LIST: Requests focus on Girdwood projects, road improvements. By ANNE AURAND Anchorage Daily News (Published: January 7, 2005) Begich Click on photo to enlarge Mayor Mark Begich has a inch-thick wish list that outlines millions of dollars of services and projects -- from roads to parks to public safety -- that the city wants the state to help pay for. Assembly members discussed the mayor's "2005 Legislative Program" Friday and are expected to make some changes before accepting it. Administration and Assembly members will lobby state lawmakers on the issues and projects laid out in the list during the legislative session that begins on Monday. For the second year in a row, the list includes an emphasis on projects in Girdwood, said David Ramseur, Begi…

title: Business owner works to broaden appeal with mix of fun activities
summary: B usiness at the Paddleboat Cafe is as slow now as it was hopping on sunny, summer days. Then, Goose Lake, in central Anchorage, was full with families basking on the beach or cruising in their rented paddleboats. This first winter Rodney Clark decided to keep the cafe open all year has dragged, gray and rainy. Anchorage's skijoring association meets at the cafe one Wednesday a month, a few skaters have come by recently, and that's about it. Taking a seasonal business year-round is always iffy, said Janet Thornton, a business counselor at Anchorage's Small Business Development Center. Research may show there just isn't an off-season market. "You're not going to be drawing the same people," Thornton said. "It's here, I have a lot of money invested in equipment," and it's fun, Clark said. Be…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Trooper Vance Peronto reported finding the five men in a 1993 GMC van driven by Alden W. Lebaron, 19, of Wasilla. Peronto stopped the van around 7 a.m. Tuesday along the Glenn Highway for an equipment violation, troopers said. The men initially told Peronto they came from Mexico and lived in California, but later admitted entering the country illegally and buying fake identification in California, troopers said. "They said for $250 they purchased a packet of identification: a counterfeit California ID, counterfeit alien registration card, counterfeit Social Security card," Staebell said. "They were fairly good quality." Lebaron, reached by phone Thursday, said he was driving the men to a job for his family's drywall company when a trooper pulled him over at the Palmer Hay Flats for having …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Anchorage-based Porcaro Communications Inc. has opened its second out-of-state office, in the Beverly Hills section of Los Angeles. This office is directed by Lisa O'Hurley, former regional director of golf sponsorships for the Golf Channel and golfchannel.com , said Michael F. Porcaro, chief executive of Porcaro Communications. The Los Angeles office will focus on golf-related clients, including apparel, courses and resort destinations, he said. His company opened a Vancouver, British Columbia, office in March 2002. People Mover ads go to Eberhardt Eberhardt Advertising was recently awarded the Anchorage People Mover advertising contract. The Anchorage company will be responsible for ad sales on the city's fleet of buses, lift vehicles and Dart buses, said Peter Eberhardt. Graphics Works …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Local Folks (Published: January 7, 2005) Alaska State Fair volunteer awards went to, from left, Rhowe, Mindy, Saige and Joel Stefanski and Kathleen Asay. Anchorage residents Lorrie, left, and Morris Horning, right, celebrate the completion of construction with new homeowner Virginia Reyes and her brother Armenio. The Hornings recently returned from Veracruz, Mexico, where they volunteered with Habitat for Humanity International's Jimmy Carter Work Project 2004. The Hornings and 1,600 volunteers from 32 countries, along with former President Carter and his wife Rosalyn, constructed 75 houses during the one-week project. This marks the 14th volunteer project that the Hornings have participated in through Habitat for Humanity, Global Volunteers and other volunteer organizations. BP Alaska hea…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Conoco, Anadarko plan satellite wells NORTH SLOPE: Sites near Alpine oil field expected to peak at 35,000 barrels of crude a day. By WESLEY LOY Anchorage Daily News (Published: December 21, 2004) Click on photo to enlarge Conoco Phillips Alaska Inc. and partner Anadarko Petroleum Corp. announced Monday they plan to develop two North Slope oil accumulations at a cost of about $450 million. The two pools will be operated as satellites to the nearby Alpine oil field. At their peak, the satellites are expected to produce about 35,000 barrels of oil per day by 2008, with first production coming in late 2006. The satellites are small as North Slope oil fields go, but Conoco believes such fields can make an important and cost-effective contribution to total North Slope production. The announcemen…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: C-5 loads up at Elmendorf Airmen from the New York Air National Guard load a C-5 Galaxy cargo plane with heavy equipment, including generators, floodlights, compressors and a warehouse crane to support the 517th Airlift squad from Elmendorf Air Force Base. The plane is bound for Jakarta, Indonesia, and will assist with relief efforts for the victims of the Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: One component of the joint agreement has not been completed -- a study of whether the lights reduce the number of moose-vehicle collisions along that stretch of the Glenn Highway. In years with heavy snowfall, according to information from the Alaska Department of Fish and Game, up to 500 migrating moose have been counted on the flats, where they enjoy easy access to the abundant shrubs in the wetlands. Migrating moose and a busy highway mean a high number of moose-vehicle run-ins. That stretch of highway ranked fifth in Alaska in 2002 for moose-vehicle collisions, according to Fish and Game. Between 1989 and 1999, about 10 collisions occurred there each year, according to the department. No study has been completed to see if the lights have reduced the number of accidents. Hay Flats refug…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: DESCRIPTION: A Siberian Yup'ik Eskimo community whose isolation has helped residents maintain their traditional culture, language and subsistence lifestyle. Residents, almost all of whom are bilingual, still use walrus-hide boats to hunt. The economy is based largely on the subsistence harvest of seal, walrus, fish, and bowhead and gray whales. Fox are trapped as a source of cash income. Some reindeer roam free, but most harvesting occurs out of the village of Savoonga. Ivory carving provides income. The abundant seabird colonies offer a chance for limited tourism. During the 2000 U.S. census, more than 28 percent of the population was living below the official U.S. poverty level. There is one school attended by about 175 students. HISTORY: St. Lawrence Island, jointly owned by Savoonga an…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Teens from Scammon Bay rap about dark side of rural life By JOSH NIVA, The Associated Press ANCHORAGE (December 31, 11:06 pm AST) - Jimmy Walker and Jaye Ulak were rappers stuck in a hip-hop no man's land. Known as Blood Family, the duo sat in the corner of a sterile conference hall in downtown Anchorage. The crowd of 30 middle-aged health workers were more likely to think of "rap" as slang for "talk." No one clapped when Walker (known as "G") and Ulak ("Blood") were introduced. The two performers moved to the front of the room. Someone pushed "play" on a boombox, and Ulak launched the blunt opening verse - without aid of a microphone - of the song "Village Issues": "I'm a survivor of drug addiction ..." The audience sat up. Mostly rural residents, these adults were witnessing something ne…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Giving from the Heart (Gift): Volunteers are needed for this holiday outreach event that provides one-stop holiday assistance to low-income individuals and families, Monday through Wednesday at the ACS garage. Daytime and evening shifts available. Last year, Gift served more than 2,700 families. (Sue Brogan, sbrogan@ak.org ) Salvation Army: Ring the bell for a two-hour shift at various sites around town for the Kettle Drive through Friday. Proceeds from the drive provide food, safe shelter and rehabilitation to Alaskans year-round. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Chuck Spinelli, president of Spinell Homes, said he's turned toward the Mat-Su area to grow his home-building business. "My bread and butter is single-family houses, and if I'm going to survive, that's where I'm going to have to do it," he said. In addition, Spinelli bought the former Legends Restaurant in Mat-Su, dropped the Legends name, and reopened for business last summer as the Settler's Bay Lodge. Spinelli says he also plans to build a hotel on the 290-acre property. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Donations of goods and services are not being accepted by the Red Cross at this time, officials said, as they could impede the distribution of priority items. Money is the most effective way to help, they said. Anyone who would like to contribute to the fund can do so at any Wells Fargo or Red Cross branch. The Alaska To Asia Disaster Relief Fund at Wells Fargo is account No. 6681201676. People who have family members missing overseas are encouraged to visit the Red Cross at http://www.icrc.org/home.nsf/home/webfamilylinks . summary: The rumor mill says Ted, Bob Penny and Bob Persons of Double Musky Inn fame are among 10 people who call themselves "Alaska's Great Eagle." title: Copyright © 2004The Anchorage Daily News
summary: ANCHORAGE Assembly votes to put onto ballot homeowner tax-exemption plan Voters in April will be able to decide whether owner-occupied homes should get a property tax exemption of 10 percent of a home's assessed value, up to $20,000. By a vote of 10-1, he Assembly on Tuesday approved putting the exemption question on the ballot. Assemblyman Dan Sullivan opposed the property tax exemption because it unfairly shifts the tax burden to commercial and rental properties and because the Assembly has provided other ways of more fairly imposing taxes, he said. Mayor Mark Begich proposed the exemption as part of this four-part tax reform plan. The Assembly already enacted a higher tobacco tax; has approved a $20,000 exemption on business personal property tax, which is the inventory of a business su…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Juneau Assembly rescinds bus fare decision The Associated Press JUNEAU (December 21, 3:42 pm AST) - The Juneau Assembly pulled its decision to collect bus fares from seniors and the disabled. The assembly decided Monday to reverse its decision. Capital Transit now will exempt seniors and low-income disabled riders. Capital Transit Manager John Kern said city buses serve about 400 seniors and people with disabilities every month. They make up 20 percent of the riders. To ride free, seniors just need to show bus drivers the sales tax exemption identification card issued by the city. Mayor Bruce Botelho said it's unclear when the new fare policy will be effective. All seniors and disabled passengers used to ride city buses for free, regardless of income, until the assembly decided this summer…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Outdoors hot line ALYESKA OFFERS SKI, SNOWBOARD CHECK SITE OF THE WEEK BORN LUCKY (Published: January 2, 2005) Tired of lugging your ski and snowboard equipment to the slopes every weekend? Alyeska has a new gear check system that allows guests to store their skis and snowboards at the hotel. The service is free for registered hotel guests and available to the public on a day-use and weekly-use basis for $5 or $20, respectively. You can also opt to have your skis waxed or tuned before your next visit. Basic tuning for skis and snowboards starts at $25, and waxing is an additional $10. For information, go to www.alyeskaresort.com or call the ski hot line at 754-7669. (Photo by Evan R. Steinhauser / Daily News archive 2002) Click on photo to enlarge ASTRONOMY Stargazers learn about and try o…

summary: Bicycle-tire studs are not created equal (Published: January 2, 2005) Steel mountain bike winter studs: left, new; right, has been used one month on pavement. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Sometimes the good deal isn't the best buy. The third smack-down on my mountain bike was enough to remind me of that axiom. Goldenview Drive had proven wet and slushy, but easily rideable. Bragaw Street had slippery spots, but nothing scary. The crossing from there onto Abbott Loop Road behind the Section 16 equestrian park had been a push through crusty old snow. Then the ride got crazy. Abbott Loop was a skating rink all the way to its intersection with Abbott Road. I no sooner got on the bike than it went down. The first fall was unexpected. So were the next …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Here is a sample of 2004 highlights: Managers and policy-makers continue to seek ways to winnow fishing fleets and boost bottom lines. Fleet reduction remained a major trend across the North Pacific, whether through fishing quota programs, cooperatives or federally funded buyouts. Buyout programs that include vessels and their catch histories were advanced for Bering Sea crab, groundfish, Bristol Bay driftnet and Southeast seine fisheries. A $100 million crab buyback program retired 25 boats from the Bering Sea crab fisheries. Alaska's salmon industry continued its slow rise from the sea bottom, brought about by its own merit, unfortunate but timely events with competitors, and lots of state and federal dollars. For the second year running, the value of the salmon catch -- 165 million fish…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: As of Monday, at least 140,000 people had been killed in 11 countries in southern Asia and East Africa. Phuket, a large island province of 1.6 million in south Thailand, was one of the hardest-hit areas. Steele; his wife, Luann McVey; his two daughters, Lydia and Laura; his in-laws, Bob and Betty McVey; his sister-in-law, Jeanie Macaulay; and her son, Liam, all from Juneau, were in the hotel. They were a steep, 150-foot climb above sea level and away from danger. A few miles away, Juneau-Douglas High School vocational teacher Craig Mapes and Dzantik'i Heeni Middle School art teacher Kathleen Wiest were uphill in the tiny town of Karon, also safe. "It was just happenstance that we weren't at the beach," said Steele, speaking on his mobile phone Thursday from the city of Kanchanburi, near th…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The resolution, if passed by the Legislature, would have to be approved by voters, which would make it a two-year process at least, Rokeberg said. "It will force the Legislature to have the discipline it needs to balance the budget," Rokeberg said. "Hopefully the people will have spoken on that and will demand that." The sponsors say a balanced budget amendment by itself is not a fiscal solution but would spark new debate on a long-range plan. And the bipartisan sponsorship of the amendment could generate the momentum to pass a plan where others have failed, they said. "It should send a signal that this is a serious proposition," Berkowitz said. "We need to do the things we agree on first. Otherwise we stagnate, and stagnation is bad for Alaska." State budget shortfalls are typically cover…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Among the eight co-authors were representatives from the Institute of Arctic Biology at the University of Alaska Fairbanks, the Marine Biological Laboratory at Woods Hole, Mass., Stanford University's Institute for International Studies, Ducks Unlimited's Institute for Wetland and Waterfowl Research in Canada and the National Wildlife Federation. The earth's climate is constantly changing, the authors point out. But hundreds of studies in the last 20 years show that human activity has contributed to global warming during the last century. The effect of rising temperatures has fallen unequally across North America. Nights have warmed more than days, while land surfaces have heated up more than ocean surfaces. Winters have warmed more than summers, the report notes, and temperatures and prec…

summary: When you've already seen it a dozen times over TROOPS: Donations of fresh DVDs and CDs are needed and much appreciated. If only there was a new movie playing on the big screen. But there is no theater, just the television and DVD player at the Morale, Welfare and Recreation facility. When Tech. Sgt. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Sales volume changed slightly for fresh pink, chum, king and silver salmon, but prices were up significantly. Most notable was fresh king salmon, which increased to $5.14 per pound from $3.35 on sales of 1.7 million pounds. For frozen salmon, the wholesale value of headed and gutted rose to $125 million, up from $67 million, mostly due to strong sales of frozen sockeye, which jumped to 55 million pounds from 26 million. Again, the average sockeye price was down to $1.74 per pound from $1.79. The May-August average prices for frozen pink and chum salmon improved, with pinks up 10 cents per pound and chums up 13 cents. Frozen headed-and-gutted silvers improved to $1.93 per pound from $1.33 on slightly higher volume of 3.5 million pounds. But the wholesale value of canned salmon fell to $51 m…

summary: • Anne Lane is now a sales associate with Dynamic Properties. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: The visitors are on package tours. While in the Fairbanks area, they will be engaged in a variety of activities, including aurora viewing, skiing and dog sled rides. Syun Akasofu, director of the University of Alaska International Arctic Research Center, also will provide a lecture on the aurora in Japanese. The flight and two more in February are sold out, said Colin Lawrence, FCVB's tourism manager. The package tours, including the flights, cost the tourists $1,200 to $1,500. The direct flight cuts in half the average amount of time it normally takes to travel between Tokyo's Narita International Airport and Fairbanks. It is the culmination of several years of negotiations between the airline, FCVB and other local representatives. For the past two years, the bureau has paid about $40,000…
